cards.back: Project reaches completion with all objectives met and deliverables accepted. Standard, successful project ending.
Project terminated before objectives are fully met. Example: discovered feasibility issues, time constraints. Partial handoff required.
Project stopped because organization's strategic priorities shifted. No longer strategic fit. Formal closeout required.
Project becomes unrecoverable (budget exhausted, timeline impossible, tech obsolete). Post-mortem to learn from failure.

cards.front: List the six wrap-up closure activities.
cards.back: 1. Obtain customer acceptance 2. Close vendor contracts 3. Release team 4. Final report 5. Archive documentation 6. Retrospective/lessons learned
Traditional approach: comprehensive review at project end documenting what was learned. Often becomes shelf-ware.
Agile approach: regular meetings after each sprint, structured, producing concrete improvement actions implemented immediately.
Leading Agile framework with roles (PO, SM, Dev team), events (sprint, daily standup, review, retro), and artifacts (backlog, increment).
Time-boxed cycle (1-4 weeks) in Scrum where team delivers working product increment. Regular heartbeat of Agile projects.

cards.front: What is the Product Owner role?
cards.back: Represents business/customer, manages and prioritizes product backlog, defines acceptance criteria, accepts completed work.

cards.front: What is the Scrum Master role?
cards.back: Facilitates Scrum process, removes blockers/impediments, coaches team on Scrum practices. NOT a traditional project manager.

cards.front: What is the Development Team role?
cards.back: Cross-functional, self-organizing group that delivers working software. Typically 3-9 people.

cards.front: How does Agile differ from Waterfall?
cards.back: Agile: iterative, adaptive, embraces change, frequent delivery. Waterfall: sequential, plan-heavy, resists change, delivers at end.
